job_summary: 3,200 sf commercial restaurant tenant improvement in Los Angeles, CA for a change of occupancy from B to A-2 assembly in a mixed-use building. This scope triggers a Building Permit for the tenant improvement/change of use, Mechanical Permit for the Type I kitchen hood and associated exhaust/grease duct work, Plumbing Permit for the grease interceptor and restroom plumbing changes, Electrical Permit for ADA restroom/path-of-travel related electrical work and any new kitchen/restroom circuits, and Fire/Life-Safety review for sprinkler modifications and hood suppression coordination. Outdoor patio seating may also trigger zoning/plan check review and possible building/fire review depending on encroachment, egress, and occupancy load.

permits_required.permit_type: Building Permit — Commercial Tenant Improvement / Change of Use (B to A-2)
permits_required.portal_selection: Commercial Tenant Improvement / Change of Use
permits_required.notes: Required for the occupancy change, tenant improvement buildout, ADA restroom/path-of-travel upgrades, and any architectural/layout work in the restaurant space.

permits_required.permit_type: Mechanical Permit — Commercial Kitchen Hood / Exhaust System
permits_required.notes: Required for the Type I hood, exhaust fan, grease duct, make-up air coordination, and any mechanical alterations tied to the restaurant kitchen.

permits_required.permit_type: Plumbing Permit — Grease Interceptor / Commercial Restroom Plumbing
permits_required.notes: Required for the grease interceptor and any restroom fixture, drain, or water line modifications tied to the tenant improvement.
Required for new or modified circuits, kitchen equipment power, restroom ADA electrical work, lighting changes, and any panel/circuit modifications.

permits_required.permit_type: Fire/Life-Safety Permit — Sprinkler Modification / Hood Suppression Coordination
permits_required.notes: Required because the scope includes sprinkler modifications and fire/life-safety review. Hood suppression may also require separate fire department review/approval depending on the final plan set.

permits_required_logic.permit_type: Building Permit — Commercial Tenant Improvement / Change of Use (B to A-2)
permits_required_logic.included_because: The job changes occupancy from Business (B) to Assembly (A-2) and includes tenant improvement work in a commercial mixed-use building, which requires building plan check and code review.
permits_required_logic.scope_trigger: Change of occupancy and commercial TI

permits_required_logic.permit_type: Mechanical Permit — Commercial Kitchen Hood / Exhaust System
permits_required_logic.included_because: A Type I kitchen hood and associated exhaust system are mechanical work and require mechanical permit review for commercial kitchen ventilation and grease duct/exhaust compliance.

permits_required_logic.permit_type: Plumbing Permit — Grease Interceptor / Commercial Restroom Plumbing
permits_required_logic.included_because: A grease interceptor is plumbing work, and restroom upgrades often involve fixture, drain, and water line changes that must be permitted.
permits_required_logic.scope_trigger: Grease interceptor and ADA restroom upgrades

permits_required_logic.included_because: Restaurant TI work commonly includes new circuits, equipment connections, lighting, and ADA restroom electrical changes; any panel or feeder changes also fall under electrical permit scope.

permits_required_logic.permit_type: Fire/Life-Safety Permit — Sprinkler Modification / Hood Suppression Coordination
permits_required_logic.included_because: The scope explicitly includes sprinkler modifications and fire/life-safety review, which triggers fire protection review and permit coordination.

applying_office: Los Angeles Department of Building and Safety (LADBS)
apply_address: 201 N. Figueroa Street, Los Angeles, CA 90012
fee_range: Fee Estimate: **$29,000-$47,500+** (structured floor — see breakdown). Components: ~$13,000 base permit + plan review (Los Angeles, CA commercial restaurant TI floor) × 1.5× jurisdiction multiplier + $3,500 change-of-occupancy adder + $5,500 hood-fire-suppression adder + $2,500 grease-interceptor adder + $4,000 fire-sprinkler-modify adder + $3,000 ada-path-of-travel adder. **Verify against current fee schedule before quoting.**

approval_timeline.simple: Over-the-counter intake can be same day to 1-3 business days for straightforward trade submittals, but this job is not simple because of change of use and commercial TI scope.
approval_timeline.complex: 3-8 weeks or longer for commercial plan check in Los Angeles, especially with change of occupancy, fire/life-safety review, ADA path of travel, and mixed-use coordination.

inspections.description: Plan checker verifies occupancy classification change to A-2, egress/occupant load, accessibility upgrades, restroom compliance, path of travel, and whether the patio seating affects occupancy or exiting.

Inspector checks Type I hood installation, exhaust fan location, grease duct routing and clearances, make-up air coordination, fire-rated shaft/penetration protection, and equipment access/clearances.
inspections.timing: Before concealment of ductwork and before hood is fully enclosed

inspections.description: Inspector verifies grease interceptor sizing/location, drain slope, fixture rough-ins, venting, cleanouts, and that restroom plumbing matches approved plans.
inspections.timing: Before slab patching, wall closure, or ceiling concealment

inspections.description: Inspector checks new circuits, conductor sizing, panel schedule changes, GFCI/AFCI where applicable, equipment disconnects, lighting controls, and ADA restroom electrical work.

Inspector or fire reviewer checks sprinkler head layout changes, hydraulic impacts, hood suppression coordination, fire alarm interface if modified, and that rated assemblies and penetrations are protected.
inspections.timing: After sprinkler modifications are installed and before final occupancy

inspections.description: Inspector verifies ADA restroom clearances, path of travel, door hardware, signage, patio seating layout, final life-safety items, and that all approved work matches the permit set.
inspections.timing: After all trades pass and before opening to the public

inspection_booking: Schedule inspections through the LADBS online permit/inspection system at https://www.ladbs.org/services/apply-for-a-permit or by calling (213) 482-0000. For commercial work, book inspections at least 24 hours in advance; plan review and field inspection coordination can take longer on multi-trade restaurant TI jobs. Have the permit number, job address, and inspection type ready when scheduling.
license_required: Licensed California contractors pull the permits on behalf of the tenant or owner: C-10 for electrical, C-36 for plumbing, C-20 for mechanical/HVAC, and the appropriate general contractor license for the building TI scope. The permit is still required; the contractor's CSLB license number and LADBS business registration information must appear on the application.
city_contractor_registration: Los Angeles City requires a CSLB license and an active Los Angeles City Business Tax Registration Certificate (BTRC) for contractors submitting permits. Verify the BTRC is current before applying; a lapsed city registration can block permit issuance or counter processing.
what_to_bring: Completed LADBS permit application and project address with suite number
CSLB license number(s) for the prime contractor and each trade contractor
Los Angeles City Business Tax Registration Certificate (BTRC)
Architectural, mechanical, plumbing, electrical, and fire protection plan set for the restaurant TI
Title 24 energy compliance documents and CF1R forms as applicable
Equipment cut sheets for the Type I hood, exhaust fan, grease interceptor, and any new kitchen equipment
common_mistakes: Submitting the restaurant TI without a full change-of-occupancy plan set — LADBS will hold the permit until A-2 occupancy, egress, and accessibility are addressed
Forgetting the Los Angeles City Business Tax Registration Certificate — LADBS can reject the application even if the CSLB license is valid
Assuming the hood suppression and sprinkler changes are covered by the building permit alone — fire/life-safety review and sprinkler permits are separate review items
Missing Title 24 energy documentation at submittal — LADBS requires CF1R compliance for HVAC-related work and may hold issuance
Starting patio seating work without checking zoning, egress, and any Bureau of Engineering encroachment issues if the patio affects public right-of-way or frontage
pro_tips: Submit the full restaurant TI package together — building, mechanical, plumbing, electrical, and fire sheets in one coordinated set reduces back-and-forth plan check corrections.
Have the BTRC and CSLB license numbers ready before opening the LADBS portal; missing contractor registration is a common reason commercial applications stall.
If the patio seating touches the sidewalk, curb lane, or any public frontage, check Bureau of Engineering review early — that can be a separate approval path from LADBS.
Include the hood manufacturer cut sheet, grease duct details, and grease interceptor sizing calculations in the first submittal; those are frequent correction items.
For ADA path-of-travel upgrades, show the accessible route, door clearances, restroom turning radius, and hardware details on the plan set so plan check doesn't bounce it.

code_citation.section: CBC 2022 Chapter 3; CBC Chapter 11B; CMC 2022 Chapter 5; CPC 2022; CEC 2022; LAMC permitting provisions
code_citation.text: Commercial tenant improvements, change of occupancy, accessibility upgrades, mechanical exhaust systems, plumbing alterations, electrical work, and fire/life-safety modifications require permit review and approval under the applicable California Building, Mechanical, Plumbing, Electrical, and Accessibility codes.

companion_permits.reason: May be required if: hood suppression, fire alarm, or sprinkler system design changes are included.
companion_permits.required_if: hood suppression, fire alarm, or sprinkler system design changes are included

companion_permits.permit_type: Bureau of Engineering / Encroachment or Public Right-of-Way Approval

companion_permits.required_if: patio seating or site work extends into public frontage/right-of-way
companion_permits.requirement_label: May be required based on scope

companion_permits.reason: May be required if: zoning review is triggered by patio seating, use intensity, or occupancy change.
companion_permits.required_if: zoning review is triggered by patio seating, use intensity, or occupancy change
companion_permits.requirement_label: May be required based on scope

zoning_hoa_flag: Outdoor patio seating in Los Angeles can trigger zoning review, occupancy load limits, outdoor dining conditions, and possibly Bureau of Engineering review if any portion encroaches into public frontage or the right-of-way. If the building is in a historic district or special overlay zone, additional approvals may apply. HOA restrictions are uncommon for a commercial tenant space but lease and landlord approvals still matter.
disclaimer: Data sourced Apr 28, 2026. Always verify current requirements directly with your local building department before starting work. Permit fees and requirements change frequently.
confidence_reason: Verified city/trade data found from official sources. Needs review for: fee_range

_meta.job_type: 3,200 sf commercial restaurant tenant improvement: change of occupancy B to A-2 assembly, Type I kitchen hood, grease interceptor, ADA restroom and path of travel upgrades, sprinkler modifications, fire/life-safety review, outdoor patio seating in a mixed-use building

fee_source.title: [PDF] 200 N. Spring Street, Room 750 • Los Angeles, CA 90012 Environme

code_section_source.title: [PDF] Title 51 PUBLIC HEALTH―SANITARY CODE Part XIV. Plumbing ...

required_documents_source.title: [PDF] Title 51 PUBLIC HEALTH―SANITARY CODE Part XIV. Plumbing ...

inspection_process_source.title: [PDF] DEPARTMENT OF - Los Angeles City Planning

checklist: Verify your contractor license is current and valid in CA
Confirm your liability insurance is active (certificate may be required at permit office)
Have the property address and legal parcel number ready
Know the project value/cost — required on most permit applications
Type I commercial exhaust hood listed/labeled per UL 710 with NFPA 96 compliance and AHJ-approved capture velocity per IMC 507
ANSUL R-102 (or equivalent UL 300) hood suppression: post-install discharge test certificate + every-6-month cleaning + annual inspection per NFPA 96 §11
ANSUL discharge auto-shutoffs all hood gas + electric fuel sources per IMC 507.7 — verify with AHJ during witness test
Grease interceptor sized per IPC 1003.3.4 by drainage fixture units (4-comp + mop typically 50+ GPM hydromechanical OR 750–1,500 gal exterior gravity); located ≤25 ft developed length from fixtures
Makeup air system balanced to ±10% of hood exhaust per IMC 506.3.4; tempered if > 35°F differential to prevent backdraft
Indirect waste with air gap on dishwasher, ice maker, prep sink, and steam kettle per IPC 802; commercial dishwasher backflow prevention (RPZ) per IPC 608.13.2
Dedicated mop sink with hose bib in janitor closet (IBC 902.3 / local health code) — required before health dept sign-off
ADA fixture counts per IBC Table 2902.1 + 60 in turning radius + lavatory pipe wrap insulation per ADA §606.5 + mirror reflective edge ≤40 in AFF per ADA §603.3
Local health department food establishment plan review + final inspection sign-off REQUIRED before Certificate of Occupancy (parallel filing — file early)
TABC / state liquor authority clearance if alcohol service — separate filing from building permit
Walk-in cooler envelope per IECC 403.10.1 (≥R-25 medium-temp panels, ≥R-32 freezer panels); door gasket seal verification at final
Existing sprinkler system reuse: hydraulic recalc for new layout + current 5-year cert per NFPA 25; new heads in cooking equipment area must be K-25+ per NFPA 13
Floor drains within 5 ft of kitchen equipment per IPC 411 with backwater valves where required
Fire alarm + emergency lighting per NFPA 72/101 with current battery capacity test on file
Change of occupancy memo (B/M → A-2): include occupant load + exits + restroom count + sprinkler/fire-alarm impacts per IEBC §1001.2/1001.3 + IBC §303.3 (Group A-2)

Maricopa County (or local equivalent) Environmental Services Department food-establishment plan review + final inspection sign-off REQUIRED before opening — coordinate with City CO schedule (MCESD: maricopa.gov)

Backflow prevention on EVERY food-service water connection: carbonated beverage (ASSE 1022 dual check), chemical dispenser at mop/service sinks, hose bibbs, dish-machine/ice/RO/espresso, pre-rinse — testable assemblies certified annually
Outdoor patio / sidewalk dining: verify city zoning (use permit may apply, e.g. Phoenix ZO §1207); include patio in occupant load + exiting + accessible route + sprinkler/awning review

Phoenix-specific (or equivalent local rule): contractor must be AZ ROC licensed AND associated with the Phoenix SHAPE PHX permit account before submittal (A.R.S. Title 32 Ch. 10) — frequently-missed pre-submittal blocker

Realistic fee warning: a 3,200 sq ft restaurant TI in Phoenix typically lands $8K–$25K+ across plan review + permit + fire + utility + health + zoning. Reject token min-fee outputs; calculate from the live fee schedule before quoting
Occupancy reclassification: identify existing approved occupancy + proposed group; require code-official approval + new/updated Certificate of Occupancy (IEBC §§1001.1, 1001.2, 1001.3; IBC Ch. 3)
Height/area limit recheck: compare new occupancy hazard category to existing construction type, allowable area, frontage/sprinkler increases, fire-wall strategy (IEBC §1011.5; IBC Ch. 5)

Fire-resistance + separation reassessment: re-verify fire barriers, horizontal assemblies, exterior wall ratings/openings, shaft enclosures, mixed-occupancy separations for the new hazard category (IEBC §§1011.1, 1011.5.3, 1011.6/7; IBC Ch. 7)
Fire protection re-run: sprinkler, fire alarm, hood suppression, and monitoring requirements at the new occupancy + IBC/IFC Ch. 9 thresholds (IEBC §§1004.1, 1011.2; IFC Ch. 9)

Means of egress recalculation: occupant load + number of exits + egress capacity + travel distance + common path + dead ends + door hardware updated to new occupancy (IEBC §§1005.1, 1011.4; IBC Ch. 10)
Accessibility / path-of-travel trigger: alterations to primary-function areas trigger ADA upgrades to altered area + serving amenities (2010 ADA §202.4 + 28 CFR §36.403)
Mechanical ventilation/exhaust recheck against the new occupancy: IMC ventilation, commercial-kitchen exhaust + makeup air, hazardous exhaust, energy compliance (IEBC §1008.1; IMC Ch. 4-6 + §507)
Plumbing demand + fixture-count recheck: water supply, sanitary load, fixture count, food-handling waste, grease/oil interceptors, chemical-waste approval (IEBC §§1009.1-1009.4; IPC Ch. 4 + 10)

Identify altered primary-function area: if scope alters an area where major activity occurs, an accessible path of travel to the altered area is required unless technically infeasible / disproportionate (2010 ADA §202.4; 28 CFR §36.403(a))
20% disproportionality cap: ADA path-of-travel work exceeding 20% of alteration cost is disproportionate at the federal level, but lower-cost items must still be prioritized (28 CFR §36.403(f)(1))

Accessible exterior route: site arrival points + accessible parking/passenger loading + curb ramps + walking surfaces + ramps/landings + slope/cross-slope + door clearances to altered area (2010 ADA §§206.2.1, 206.4, 403, 404, 405, 406)

Restroom path + restroom upgrades: if restrooms serve the altered area, accessible route + door + turning space + lavatory + water closet + grab bars + clearances + signage (2010 ADA §§213, 603-606, 609, 703)
Parking + passenger loading: recalculate accessible-stall count + van space + access aisle + signage for affected parking serving the altered area (2010 ADA §§208, 502, 503; 28 CFR §36.403(e)(1))
Drinking fountains, phones, and signage along the path: tactile/visual signs, room ID, directional, fountain units, public phones if any (2010 ADA §§211, 216, 602, 703, 704)
Doors, thresholds, and hardware on the route: clear width + maneuvering clearance + opening force + thresholds + lever/operable hardware + protruding objects (2010 ADA §§303, 307, 309, 404)

Confirm the required license is active and matches the application: Licensed California contractors pull the permits on behalf of the tenant or owner: C-10 for electrical, C-36 for plumbing, C-20 for mechanical/HVAC, and the appropriate general contractor license for the building TI scope. The permit is still required; the contractor's CSLB license number and LADBS business registration information must appear on the application.
Bring 2 copies of all documents to Los Angeles Department of Building and Safety (LADBS)

rejection_patterns.pattern: Missing CSLB license AND LA City Business Tax Registration Certificate
rejection_patterns.fix: Both required — get BTRC at finance.lacity.org before permit application

rejection_patterns.pattern: Missing CF1R energy compliance form for HVAC replacements
rejection_patterns.fix: Generate CF1R through a certified HERS rater before applying

rejection_patterns.pattern: Bureau of Engineering approval missing for projects near public streets
rejection_patterns.fix: Submit to Bureau of Engineering separately from Building & Safety

fee_calculator.note: Provide job_value to calculate an exact fee where formulas are available.

expert_notes.title: California ADU 60-day ministerial shot clock
expert_notes.note: California state law (AB 881 / Govt Code 65852.2) requires the city to approve or deny ADU permits within 60 days of a complete application. If they exceed this, you have grounds to escalate.

expert_notes.title: California ADU impact-fee exemption under 750 sq ft
expert_notes.note: Impact fees waived for ADUs under 750 sq ft per California Government Code 65852.2(f)(3).

expert_notes.title: Title 24 / CF1R energy compliance
expert_notes.note: Title 24 energy compliance and CF1R documentation are required for new construction and alterations that change conditioned space. Solar mandate may apply for new detached ADUs depending on scope.
expert_notes.applies_to: New construction, ADUs, and conditioned-space alterations

expert_notes.title: Cal Fire VHFHSZ check
expert_notes.note: Check whether the parcel is in a Cal Fire Very High Fire Hazard Severity Zone (VHFHSZ). Pasadena, Malibu, Santa Barbara, and parts of Los Angeles County can trigger additional fire-resistive material and defensible-space requirements.

expert_notes.title: CSLB licensing — $500 unlicensed-work threshold
expert_notes.note: California Contractors State License Board (CSLB) requires a license for any single project with combined labor + materials at $500 or more. Unlicensed contracting above that threshold is a misdemeanor (B&P §7028). Verify license + classification (e.g. B General Building, C-10 Electrical, C-36 Plumbing, C-20 HVAC) on the CSLB lookup before quoting; expired or suspended licenses void the contract and block lien rights.
expert_notes.applies_to: All paid construction work in California ≥ $500

expert_notes.title: SB 9 lot split + 2-unit ministerial path
expert_notes.note: SB 9 (Govt Code §65852.21 / §66411.7) lets owners of single-family-zoned parcels split a lot into two and/or build a duplex per resulting parcel under ministerial review (no public hearing, no CEQA). Combined with ADU/JADU rules this can yield up to 4 units on a former SFR lot. Owner must sign a 3-year owner-occupancy affidavit on lot splits. Cities can impose objective design standards but cannot deny qualifying SB 9 projects.
expert_notes.applies_to: Single-family-zoned residential infill and duplex conversions

expert_notes.title: California Building Standards Code triennial cycle (2025 → 2026 enforcement)
expert_notes.note: California adopts a new Title 24 / CBSC edition every three years. The 2025 California Building/Residential/Plumbing/Electrical/Mechanical/Energy/Green Building Standards Codes were published Jul 1, 2025 and become enforceable Jan 1, 2026 statewide. Permit applications submitted before Jan 1, 2026 may still be reviewed under the 2022 code at the AHJ's discretion — confirm which code edition applies before submitting drawings.
expert_notes.applies_to: Permit applications crossing the 2026-01-01 code-change boundary

expert_notes.title: CALGreen mandatory + EV-ready / solar-ready requirements
expert_notes.note: CALGreen (Title 24 Part 11) sets statewide green-building minimums: low-VOC finishes, water-use reduction (≤1.28 gpf toilets, ≤1.8 gpm kitchen faucets), EV-ready raceway in new SFR garages, and solar-ready zone for new low-rise residential. Tier 1/Tier 2 voluntary measures may be locally mandated (e.g., San Francisco, Santa Monica). Plan check rejection is common when the EV raceway or solar-ready zone is omitted from new SFR/ADU drawings.
expert_notes.applies_to: New construction and major remodels statewide

expert_notes.title: California municipal utility coordination
expert_notes.note: Local utility is Los Angeles Department of Water and Power (LADWP) — service/interconnection coordination goes through the city utility, not SoCal Edison/PG&E.
expert_notes.applies_to: Electrical service, solar, battery, EV, and utility-interconnection work

expert_notes.title: Local VHFHSZ risk flag
expert_notes.note: Los Angeles may include Very High Fire Hazard Severity Zone parcels. Verify the address on the Cal Fire VHFHSZ map before quoting exterior materials or ADU/solar scope.

hidden_triggers.title: Change of occupancy (B/S to A-2/M) can trigger sprinkler retrofit and IEBC review
hidden_triggers.why_it_matters: Converting an office, warehouse, or retail tenant to assembly or mercantile crosses occupancy classifications. The new occupant load can cross IBC 903.2 sprinkler thresholds and require an IEBC change-of-occupancy analysis even when the construction work looks small.
likely_required_actions: Run IEBC change-of-occupancy analysis (Chapter 10)
Recompute occupant load and check IBC 903.2 sprinkler thresholds
Update accessible route and restroom/fixture counts to the higher occupancy
Coordinate fire-alarm and egress lighting for the proposed occupancy
companion_permits: Fire sprinkler permit if retrofit/relocation triggered
Fire alarm permit if devices/coverage change
citations: IEBC Chapter 10 [verify adopted edition before merging]

hidden_triggers.title: Grease interceptor / FOG review may be required for commercial food prep
hidden_triggers.why_it_matters: FOG requirements are often enforced by sewer/environmental agencies outside the normal building-permit checklist. Missing sizing or location approval can force plumbing redesign.
likely_required_actions: Size interceptor from fixture/equipment schedule
Confirm location/access with sewer or environmental authority
Show sampling port/cleanout details if required
companion_permits: FOG / industrial waste / sewer approval, if required

hidden_triggers.title: Restaurant seating changes require occupant-load and egress recalculation
hidden_triggers.why_it_matters: Adding dining, bar, queueing, or patio seats can change the required number of exits, door swing, panic hardware, travel distance, restroom counts, and fire-alarm assumptions.
likely_required_actions: Add occupant-load table by room/area
Show common path, exit access travel distance, and exit count
Coordinate exit signage/emergency lighting

hidden_triggers.title: Restaurant work needs local health-department food-establishment plan review
hidden_triggers.why_it_matters: Health-department review usually runs in parallel with the building permit and approves finish schedule, hand sinks, three-compartment sink, warewashing, refrigeration, and food-flow. Missing it can hold the certificate of occupancy even when building review is finished.
likely_required_actions: Submit food-establishment plans and equipment schedule to local health department
Show finish schedule, hand sinks, mop sink, three-compartment / warewashing, refrigeration
Coordinate health inspection before final CO
citations: FDA Food Code as adopted by local health authority [verify edition before merging]

hidden_triggers.title: Restroom alteration can trigger ADA path-of-travel upgrades
hidden_triggers.why_it_matters: Altering a primary-function area or restroom can require upgrades to the accessible route, restrooms, drinking fountains, telephones, and parking up to the 20% disproportionality cap.
likely_required_actions: Prepare ADA path-of-travel cost allocation
Show accessible route to altered area
Verify restroom clearances, accessories, lavatory, mirror, and door hardware

hidden_triggers.title: Commercial alteration can require accessible-route/path-of-travel upgrades
hidden_triggers.why_it_matters: Even when the requested work is not 'an ADA project,' altered primary-function areas can require accessible route, restroom, drinking fountain, parking, and entrance upgrades within the disproportionality cap.
likely_required_actions: Prepare path-of-travel analysis
Budget required accessible upgrades
Document 20% disproportionality cap if used

hidden_triggers.title: Occupancy or occupant-load change can alter required restroom fixture counts
hidden_triggers.why_it_matters: A retail/office/assembly change may look like tenant buildout but can require additional fixtures or accessible upgrades if occupant load or use category changes.
likely_required_actions: Prepare occupant-load and plumbing-fixture-count table
Check accessible fixture requirements
Coordinate any restroom expansion with path-of-travel obligations

hidden_triggers.title: Restaurant occupancy or suppression work may require fire-alarm interface review
hidden_triggers.why_it_matters: Suppression systems, duct detectors, occupant load, and sprinkler changes can all require alarm interlocks, monitoring updates, or a separate fire-alarm submittal.
likely_required_actions: Confirm whether alarm shop drawings are deferred or separate
Show suppression/alarm interface sequence
Update monitoring account and acceptance testing
companion_permits: Fire alarm permit, if devices/interface change

hidden_triggers.title: Restaurant seat count can change required plumbing fixtures
hidden_triggers.why_it_matters: Dining, bar, patio, and assembly occupant loads can push the project over restroom fixture thresholds, affecting cost, space planning, and accessibility upgrades.
likely_required_actions: Prepare occupant-load and fixture-count table
Verify male/female or all-gender fixture distribution rules
Coordinate accessible restroom clearances

_fee_floor_components.scope_note: plan review + permit + fire + utility + health
_fee_floor_components.jurisdiction_rationale: LADBS + BOE/LAFD/Planning overlays; high cost-of-government metro [verify current LADBS fee estimator]
